evals.prompt: Break down the PRD at /Users/cartergran/Documents/agent-arsenal.nosync/PRD.md into technical implementation tasks for the agents who will build it. Group tasks by agent track and give each agent enough context to work independently.
evals.expected_output: A structured markdown document with at least two agent tracks (Frontend/React Native and Database/Supabase), each with multiple numbered tasks. Tasks reference FR-xxx numbers, include relevant data model excerpts, have acceptance criteria, and note dependencies. The document includes a shared context block per track covering the tech stack and relevant constraints.

assertions.name: tasks-have-acceptance-criteria

assertions.name: tasks-have-dependencies

assertions.name: has-shared-context-per-track
assertions.description: Each track section includes a shared context block covering relevant stack details or constraints

assertions.name: covers-p0-requirements
assertions.description: Output covers at least auth (FR-001–003), card stack (FR-006–008), swipe actions (FR-012–015), and feed (FR-021–023)

assertions.name: tasks-are-implementation-sized
assertions.description: Tasks are scoped to 0.5–2 days of work, not mega-tasks like 'build the entire app'

evals.prompt:
I have a PRD for a JWT-based authentication system for our Node.js/Express SaaS app. Here it is:

# PRD: User Authentication System

**Status**: Draft
**Last Updated**: 2026-04-17
**Version**: 1.0

## 1. Overview
**What**: A JWT-based authentication system for a SaaS web app. Users can sign up with email/password, log in, reset their password via email, and authenticate via Google OAuth.
**Why**: The app currently has no authentication. All data is unprotected.
**Who**: End users of the SaaS product.

## 2. Goals & Success Metrics
1. Registration-to-first-login conversion ≥ 80%
2. Login endpoint p99 latency < 150ms
3. Zero PII stored outside the designated auth tables

## 3. Scope
**In Scope**: Email/password signup and login, JWT issuance (access + refresh tokens), password reset via email, Google OAuth, middleware to protect existing routes.
**Out of Scope**: SSO/SAML, role-based access control, multi-tenancy, 2FA.

## 4. User Stories
1. As a new user, I want to sign up with my email and password so I can access the app.
2. As a returning user, I want to log in and have my session persist for 7 days.
3. As a user who forgot my password, I want to receive a reset link via email.
4. As a user, I want to log in with my Google account.

## 5. Functional Requirements
**FR-001** [P0] — POST /auth/register accepts { email, password }. Creates a user record, hashes the password with bcrypt (cost factor ≥ 12), and returns a signed access token (15-min expiry) and a refresh token (7-day expiry).
**FR-002** [P0] — POST /auth/login accepts { email, password }. Returns same token pair as FR-001 on success. Returns 401 with generic error message on failure (no user enumeration).
**FR-003** [P0] — POST /auth/refresh accepts a valid refresh token and returns a new access token. Invalidates the old refresh token (rotation).
**FR-004** [P0] — Express middleware verifies the access token on all protected routes. Returns 401 if missing/expired/invalid.
**FR-005** [P1] — POST /auth/forgot-password accepts { email }. If the email exists, sends a reset link with a short-lived signed token (1-hour expiry). Always returns 200 to prevent enumeration.
**FR-006** [P1] — POST /auth/reset-password accepts { token, newPassword }. Validates the token, hashes the new password, invalidates the token, and updates the user record.
**FR-007** [P1] — GET /auth/google and GET /auth/google/callback implement Google OAuth via Passport.js. On success, upserts the user record and issues the same token pair.
**FR-008** [P0] — Refresh tokens are stored in a database table (not in-memory) so they survive server restarts and can be revoked.

## 6. Non-Functional Requirements
- Login endpoint p99 < 150ms
- Passwords stored only as bcrypt hashes; plaintext never logged
- Access tokens: short-lived (15 min). Refresh tokens: rotated on every use.

## 7. Technical Constraints & Assumptions
- Stack: Node.js, Express, PostgreSQL, Passport.js for OAuth
- JWT library: jsonwebtoken
- Email: Nodemailer + SendGrid
- Existing routes are Express middleware chains; auth middleware plugs in as a standard Express middleware

## 8. Data Model
### users
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| id | uuid PK | |
| email | text UNIQUE NOT NULL | |
| password_hash | text | null for OAuth-only users |
| google_id | text | null for email/password users |
| created_at | timestamptz | |

### refresh_tokens
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| id | uuid PK | |
| user_id | uuid FK → users | |
| token_hash | text UNIQUE | bcrypt hash of the raw token |
| expires_at | timestamptz | |
| revoked | boolean | default false |
| created_at | timestamptz | |

### password_reset_tokens
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| id | uuid PK | |
| user_id | uuid FK → users | |
| token_hash | text UNIQUE | |
| expires_at | timestamptz | |
| used | boolean | default false |

## 9. Open Questions
1. Should we enforce unique emails across OAuth and email/password accounts, or allow the same email to exist with both login methods?
2. What is the token payload structure — should we include roles now even though RBAC is out of scope?

Break this into implementation tasks for each agent track.
evals.expected_output: A structured breakdown with at least two tracks: Backend (Node.js/Express) and Database (PostgreSQL). Backend tasks cover the API endpoints, JWT logic, OAuth, and auth middleware. Database tasks cover schema migrations and indexes. Tasks reference FR numbers, include relevant schema/query context, have acceptance criteria, and note the open questions about email uniqueness and token payload.

assertions.name: has-backend-and-database-tracks
assertions.description: Output has separate Backend (or Node.js/Express) and Database (or PostgreSQL) tracks

assertions.name: tasks-reference-fr-numbers
assertions.description: Tasks reference FR-xxx numbers from the PRD

assertions.name: tasks-have-acceptance-criteria
assertions.description: At least half the tasks have explicit acceptance criteria

assertions.name: covers-all-endpoints
assertions.description: Tasks collectively cover register, login, refresh, auth middleware, forgot-password, reset-password, and Google OAuth

assertions.name: database-tasks-cover-schema
assertions.description: Database track includes tasks for users table, refresh_tokens table, and password_reset_tokens table

assertions.name: surfaces-open-questions
assertions.description: At least one open question from the PRD (email uniqueness or token payload) is surfaced in a relevant task

assertions.name: security-requirements-propagated
assertions.description: Security NFRs (bcrypt cost factor, no plaintext logging, token rotation) appear in relevant task context

evals.prompt:
We finished writing the PRD for our real-time notification feature. It's a React frontend with a Node/Express backend and PostgreSQL. Here's the spec:

# PRD: Real-Time Notifications

**Status**: Draft
**Last Updated**: 2026-04-17
**Version**: 1.0

## 1. Overview
**What**: In-app real-time notifications delivered via WebSocket. Users receive notifications when someone comments on their post, mentions them (@username), or likes their content. Notifications appear as a badge count on a bell icon and in a dropdown panel.
**Why**: Users miss engagement on their content because there's no real-time signal.
**Who**: All authenticated users of the platform.

## 2. Goals & Success Metrics
1. Notification delivery latency < 2 seconds (p95) from event to client display
2. Notification badge renders within 200ms of login for users with unread notifications
3. ≥ 95% notification delivery rate for online users

## 3. Scope
**In Scope**: WebSocket connection management, three notification types (comment, mention, like), unread badge count, notification dropdown panel, mark-as-read (individual and bulk).
**Out of Scope**: Push notifications (mobile/desktop), email digests, notification preferences/muting, pagination beyond 50 most recent.

## 4. User Stories
1. As a user, I want to see a badge count update in real time when I receive new notifications.
2. As a user, I want to open a notification panel and see my recent notifications.
3. As a user, I want to click a notification and be taken to the relevant content.
4. As a user, I want to mark all notifications as read with one click.

## 5. Functional Requirements
**FR-001** [P0] — The backend exposes a WebSocket endpoint at /ws. Authenticated clients connect by including their JWT in the connection handshake (Authorization header or query param).
**FR-002** [P0] — When a comment, mention, or like event fires in the backend, the notification service looks up the recipient's active WebSocket connection and delivers a notification payload: { id, type, actor_id, actor_name, content_id, content_preview, created_at, read: false }.
**FR-003** [P0] — The notification service writes every notification to the notifications table before attempting WebSocket delivery. If the recipient is offline, the notification is persisted and delivered on next connection.
**FR-004** [P0] — On connection, the server sends the client their 50 most recent notifications and the current unread count.
**FR-005** [P0] — POST /notifications/:id/read marks a single notification as read (sets read = true, read_at = now()).
**FR-006** [P0] — POST /notifications/read-all marks all of the authenticated user's unread notifications as read.
**FR-007** [P1] — The React frontend displays a bell icon in the top nav with a badge showing the unread count. The badge uses a red dot style for 1–9 and "9+" for 10 or more.
**FR-008** [P1] — Clicking the bell opens a dropdown panel (max height 400px, scrollable) listing the 50 most recent notifications. Each row shows: actor avatar, summary text (e.g., "Alice commented on your post"), relative timestamp, and a blue dot indicator if unread.
**FR-009** [P1] — Clicking a notification row marks it as read and navigates to the relevant content (post, comment, or profile).
**FR-010** [P1] — A "Mark all as read" button at the top of the dropdown calls POST /notifications/read-all and clears all blue dot indicators.
**FR-011** [P0] — The unread count in the bell badge is decremented in real time as individual notifications are marked read.

## 6. Non-Functional Requirements
- WebSocket connections must authenticate; unauthenticated connections are rejected immediately.
- The notification service must handle 10,000 concurrent WebSocket connections.
- Notification delivery latency < 2s (p95).
- Notifications table must be indexed on (user_id, read, created_at) for efficient unread queries.

## 7. Technical Constraints
- Frontend: React, existing Redux store for global state
- Backend: Node.js, Express, ws library for WebSocket
- Database: PostgreSQL
- Existing event hooks are already in place for comment, mention, and like events — the notification service needs to hook into these

## 8. Data Model
### notifications
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| id | uuid PK | |
| user_id | uuid FK → users | recipient |
| type | text | 'comment', 'mention', 'like' |
| actor_id | uuid FK → users | who triggered it |
| content_id | uuid | the post/comment being reacted to |
| content_preview | text | short excerpt, max 100 chars |
| read | boolean | default false |
| read_at | timestamptz | null if unread |
| created_at | timestamptz | |

## 9. Open Questions
1. How should we handle notification delivery if the server restarts mid-connection? Do we need Redis pub/sub for multi-instance deployments?
2. Should the content_id be polymorphic (pointing to either posts or comments), or should we add a content_type field?

Create implementation tasks from this PRD, grouped by agent track.
evals.expected_output: A structured breakdown with Frontend (React), Backend (Node.js/Express/WebSocket), and Database (PostgreSQL) tracks. Backend tasks cover the WebSocket endpoint, connection management, notification service integration with existing event hooks, and read endpoints. Frontend tasks cover the bell icon/badge, dropdown panel, real-time state updates, and mark-as-read. Database tasks cover schema and indexes. Open questions about Redis and content_type are surfaced. Performance NFRs (10k connections, 2s latency) appear in relevant tasks.

assertions.name: has-three-tracks
assertions.description: Output has Frontend, Backend, and Database tracks

assertions.name: tasks-reference-fr-numbers
assertions.description: Tasks reference FR-xxx numbers from the PRD

assertions.name: tasks-have-acceptance-criteria
assertions.description: At least half the tasks include explicit acceptance criteria

assertions.name: backend-covers-websocket
assertions.description: Backend track includes tasks for WebSocket endpoint authentication and connection management

assertions.name: frontend-covers-bell-and-panel
assertions.description: Frontend track includes tasks for the bell badge (unread count) and notification dropdown panel

assertions.name: database-covers-schema-and-indexes
assertions.description: Database track includes tasks covering the notifications table schema and the composite index on (user_id, read, created_at)

assertions.name: surfaces-redis-question
assertions.description: The open question about Redis pub/sub for multi-instance deployments is surfaced in a relevant task
